Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic most of the time. I work part-time to pay my bills, so I had to squeeze studying into whatever gaps I could find between shifts. I'd usually try to watch lecture videos late at night when things were quieter, but I often found myself too exhausted to really absorb everything. I made visual notes and diagrams when I could because that's how things stick for me, but organizing a consistent routine felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials because work picked up or I was dealing with personal stuff that drained me emotionally. Then when deadlines approached, I'd have these intense cramming sessions where I'd spend hours trying to catch up. It wasn't healthy, but it was survival mode most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder that made me panic, or this nagging guilt that I was falling behind again. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were new announcements or if I'd missed something important. The peer support group I was connected with would sometimes message me to check in, and that would prompt me to actually open the LMS and see what I needed to do.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became more automatic, usually when I was doing better mentally and had more energy. I'd set it as my homepage for a while so it was the first thing I saw when I opened my browser. But honestly, maintaining that routine was hard when my anxiety spiked or when work got overwhelming. The habit would slip away and I'd have to rebuild it again.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I'm a visual learner and I could pause, rewind, and really process what was being shown. I watched almost 300 minutes of video content because that format just clicks for me. The discussion forums were also helpful because seeing other students' questions made me feel less alone in my confusion. When someone explained a concept differently in the forums, sometimes it finally made sense to me.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials, especially the dense PDFs that were just walls of text. With my visual learning style, those documents felt like torture to get through. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions beyond the minimum because I felt embarrassed about how behind I was. It's hard to collaborate when you feel like you're the weakest link in the group.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to the exams or assignments. I needed that clear payoff because my time and energy were so limited. If something felt like busywork or wasn't going to help my grade, I couldn't justify spending my precious study hours on it. Visual demonstrations and worked examples were always worth it because they helped concepts click faster.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I knew they might help, but with everything else I was juggling, they always fell to the bottom of the list. Required activities at least gave me a clear target and deadline to work toward. Optional stuff just added to my anxiety because I knew I should do it but couldn't find the bandwidth, and then I'd feel guilty about skipping it.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of lecture slides and video files to study offline because my internet at home isn't always reliable. I'd print out diagrams and create my own visual summaries that I'd review during my breaks at work. I also used YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials alone. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was real studying.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting at work during slow periods, going through my printed notes or reviewing diagrams I'd made on paper. I'd also spend time just thinking through problems in my head while commuting or doing mindless tasks. Sometimes I'd have study sessions with the one peer I connected with through the support program, and we'd work through problems together in person. The LMS had no idea any of that was happening.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty common for me. I'd download a video or PDF once, and then that file lived on my phone or laptop for weeks. I'd watch the same lecture video multiple times offline, especially before exams. The LMS probably showed I accessed it once, but I might have reviewed that same content five or six times on my own device.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where engaging felt almost impossible. When my anxiety was really high, just opening the LMS made my chest tight. I had some family issues this semester that left me feeling really sad and drained for weeks. During those times, even logging in felt like climbing a mountain, and I'd fall further behind which just made everything worse.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Working to support myself while studying meant I was always tired and stretched thin. When assignments piled up and I felt confused about the material, I'd sometimes just shut down instead of asking for help. The fatigue made it hard to concentrate even when I did find time to study, so I'd spend hours on the LMS without really absorbing anything.
Q14: This happened more than I'd like to admit. I'd have the videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or replaying stressful conversations. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. I was physically present on the platform but mentally I was checked out because I was too overwhelmed to truly focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were hurting my grade. Part of it was anxiety—starting something felt harder than avoiding it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy, but tomorrow I'd feel just as drained. The assignments I completed were only about 31% of them, which honestly reflects how much I was struggling to just get things done.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my only motivator. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything because my self-discipline was shot from exhaustion. But they also caused massive stress spikes that made my anxiety worse. I'd often submit things at the last possible minute, rushing through work that deserved more time and attention.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially encouraging feedback, it gave me a small boost to keep going. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback right away because I was scared of what it might say. The peer support intervention helped because having someone check in on me made me feel accountable. That human connection mattered more than automated feedback from the system.
Q18: The structure with eleven online courses was honestly overwhelming for someone in my situation. Having so much content available was good in theory, but it also made it easy to fall behind without realizing how deep the hole was getting. The flexibility was necessary for my work schedule, but it also meant I had to be my own taskmaster, which didn't work well when I was depressed or anxious.
Q19: The video-heavy format helped me a lot because of how I learn best. But the sheer volume of content and the pace of new materials being released made it hard to keep up. Having clearer milestones or check-ins might have helped me stay on track. The peer support was a good design choice—that human element made a difference even when I was struggling.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. High login frequency and video watching, but low assignment completion. For me, it was about the gap between consuming content and producing work. Watching videos felt manageable, but actually doing assignments required energy and focus I often didn't have. The anxiety of starting something and possibly failing was sometimes worse than just not doing it.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies efficiently offline, downloads what they need, and focuses intensely for short periods, their LMS activity would look low but they might still learn the material. For me, my exam score was decent at 67 even though my assignment completion was poor, partly because I did a lot of studying that wasn't captured online.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on videos. But they can't show understanding or emotional state. They can't see that I watched a video three times offline or that I was crying while trying to study. They miss the human struggle behind the numbers and the real learning that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it, even when it's hard. For me, engagement isn't just about logging in or watching videos—it's about wrestling with concepts until they make sense. It's showing up mentally even when you're exhausted and scared. I was engaged this semester, just not in ways that always showed up in the data.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the nights I stayed up studying after work shifts, or the anxiety attacks I pushed through to submit assignments. It wouldn't capture the sadness I carried or the resilience it took to keep going when dropping out would have been easier. I passed this course, and that pass represents more struggle than any login count could ever reveal.
